Individuals with Disabilities Education Act Section 618 · Parts B and C

IDEA Section 618 Data Tool

Explore national trends, State snapshots, side-by-side comparisons, and the full set of data collections, all drawn from the public data that every State reports each year to the U.S. Department of Education's Office of Special Education Programs under the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA), Section 618.

National Trends

Children and Students With Disabilities Served Under IDEA Part B, Ages 3–21, 1976–2025

This chart illustrates the consistent and significant growth in the number of children and students with disabilities served under IDEA Part B, ages 3–21, from School Year 1976–77 to today. Select one or more primary disability categories below to compare each category with the overall total.

Each Primary Disability Category at a Glance

A quick view of every primary disability category, showing the most recent count of children and students served and how much it has changed since the category was first reported under IDEA Part B.
Share of total

Each Category as a Share of the Total

Each selected primary disability category shown as its share of all children and students with disabilities served under IDEA Part B, ages 3–21, in School Year 2024–25.
Change

Largest Changes in Count by Category

The primary disability categories with the largest increases and decreases in the number of children and students served, shown in thousands, through School Year 2024–25.
Source: U.S. Department of Education, National Center for Education Statistics, Digest of Education Statistics 2023, Table 204.30; and Office of Special Education Programs, “IDEA Part B Child Count and Educational Environments Collection,” School Years 1976–77 through 2024–25.

Disability Category Explorer

Choose one primary disability category to see how many children and students it serves, where they spend the school day, who they are, and how the numbers have changed over time. All figures cover children and students served under IDEA Part B, ages 3–21, across the 50 States and the District of Columbia.

Display
Trend

Child Count Trend

Number of children and students served under IDEA Part B each school year (ages 3–21), shown in thousands across the 50 states and DC. The line traces how the count for the selected disability category has changed over time; switch to Percent above to view each year as a share of all students served.
Environment

Educational Environments, Part B, Ages 5–21, School Year 2024–25

Where school-aged children and students with disabilities in this category spend most of the school day, ages 5–21, in School Year 2024–25. Higher shares inside the regular class reflect more inclusive educational environments.
Demographics

Sex, Part B, Ages 3–21, School Year 2024–25

Children and students served under IDEA Part B by reported sex.
Demographics

Race and Ethnicity, Part B, Ages 3–21, School Year 2024–25

Children and students served under IDEA Part B by race and ethnicity.
Age

Age, Part B, Ages 3–21, School Year 2024–25

Children and students served under IDEA Part B by single year of age.
Primary classification. These counts reflect each child's single primary disability category as recorded on the individualized education program (IEP), not clinical prevalence, and each child is counted only once. This applies to every category shown. For example, the autism category includes only children whose primary IDEA category is autism; it does not include every child with an autism diagnosis (a child served primarily under another category, such as intellectual disability or speech or language impairment, is counted there instead), nor children who are not served under IDEA.
Source: U.S. Department of Education, Office of Special Education Programs, IDEA Section 618 Child Count and Educational Environments Collection, School Year 2024–25 (50 States and the District of Columbia). The 50-year trend draws on the National Center for Education Statistics, Digest of Education Statistics 2023, Table 204.30, together with the Office of Special Education Programs 2023–24 and 2024–25 files. Counts, demographics, and ages cover children and students served, ages 3–21; educational environments cover school-aged students, ages 5–21. Suppressed small cells are treated as zero, so a few totals are marginal undercounts.

Side-by-Side Comparison

Place two primary disability categories or two States next to each other to see how they compare today and how each has changed over time. Every chart names the IDEA part, age group, and school year it covers.

Display

Trend

Source: U.S. Department of Education, Office of Special Education Programs (OSEP), IDEA Section 618 collections (50 states + DC). Category counts reflect each child's single primary disability category as recorded on the IEP.

State Snapshot

See how any State compares with the rest of the nation. This page brings together the State's IDEA Section 618 data, the U.S. Department of Education's annual determination of how well the State meets IDEA requirements, an interactive U.S. map, and State-by-State rankings.

SectionsSnapshotMapSection 618SPP/APR

Child Count Trend

IDEA Part B Child Count and Educational Environments Collection, ages 3–21.
Scope
The U.S. map and the State rankings cover the 50 States and the District of Columbia. Additional IDEA reporting entities, Puerto Rico, the Bureau of Indian Education, the outlying areas, and the freely associated states, appear in the selector when data are available, but they are shown separately and are not included in the 50 States and DC rankings. Each measure is labeled by collection, IDEA part, age group, and school year.

Map and Rankings

Choose one measure to color the map and rank the States.

U.S. Map,

Hover over any State to see its value. Click a State to load it into this page.

State Rankings

Source: U.S. Department of Education, Office of Special Education Programs, IDEA Section 618 collections.

Section 618 Measures

About these measures
Each measure is drawn from the IDEA Section 618 data collections and is labeled with its IDEA part, age group, and school year. Each tile shows this State's value, the national value, and the State's rank.

Measures by collection

How ranks are calculated
Ranks are out of the reporting units included for each measure. Most Part B child count and educational environment ranks use the 50 States and the District of Columbia. Graduation, dropout, and certification exclude New Mexico when data are suppressed for quality. Assessment proficiency is shown only in the assessment section and is not ranked here because state assessment systems are not directly comparable across states.

Trends, comparison & all States

How this State's published Part B measures compare with the nation, its child-count trend over time, and a sortable table of all States.

Data Collections

The Office of Special Education Programs (OSEP), within the Office of Special Education and Rehabilitative Services (OSERS), is dedicated to improving results for infants, toddlers, children, and youth with disabilities, ages birth through 21, by providing leadership and financial support to assist States/entities, local districts, and programs. Section 618 of IDEA requires that each State/entity submit data about the infants and toddlers, birth through age 2, who receive early intervention services under Part C of IDEA, and students with disabilities, ages 3 through 21, who receive special education and related services under Part B of IDEA.

The full set of public IDEA Section 618 data collections, organized by reporting level, IDEA part, collection, and school year. Choose a collection to view its tables, charts, and original source files.

Browse IDEA Section 618 collections

Choose a collection to view available tables, graphs, or source files.

Source note
U.S. Department of Education, Office of Special Education Programs: IDEA Part B Discipline, Exiting, Personnel, and Assessment collections, School Year 2023–24; IDEA Part C Child Count and Settings, School Year 2024–25; and IDEA Part C Exiting, School Year 2023–24.
Graphs
Charts and visual summaries for the selected collection, when available.
Part B Ages 3–21
Part B · Discipline · School Year 2023–24

Disciplinary Removals, Part B Discipline Collection, Ages 3–21, School Year 2023–24

910,436 distinct students with disabilities (≈12%, about 1 in 8) had at least one disciplinary removal. Because a student can be removed more than once, the total number of removal events was far higher, about 2.24 million. This chart counts each student once, by their cumulative days removed. Per Office of Special Education Programs documentation: the unduplicated student count comes from file FS088/DG598; the 2.24M “times removed” figure from FS143/DG682.

Removal Event Measures

Selected event records from the discipline files.
Part B · Exiting · School Year 2023–24

Exiting Special Education, Part B Exiting Collection, Ages 14–21, School Year 2023–24

How students with disabilities, ages 14–21, left special education in School Year 2023–24, shown as a percent of all students reported in the Exiting collection. Graduating with a regular high school diploma is the most common reason students exit.

Grouped Exit Outcomes

Grouped view of the same Exiting collection.

FTE counts by qualification status.

Assessment Proficiency, Part B Assessment Collection, School Year 2023–24

National aggregate proficiency for participating students with IEPs. Do not use these bars for state-to-state comparisons.

Participants and Proficiency

Participant counts and the subset proficient or above.

Sources, data notes & suppression key
U.S. Department of Education · Office of Special Education Programs
IDEA Section 618 Data Products
Child count, educational environments, discipline, exiting, personnel, assessment, and the Part C collections, the core of this data tool (50 states + DC).
School Years 1976–77 through 2024–25
NCES
Digest of Education Statistics 2023
Historical national and state child-count series (Tables 204.30, 204.65, 204.70), drawn from the Office of Special Education Programs IDEA database.
1976–77 through 2022–23
NCES · NAEP
The Nation's Report Card (NAEP)
Long-Term Trend (ages 9 and 13) and Main NAEP results for students with disabilities compared with peers (national sample).
2012 through 2024
U.S. Census Bureau
American Community Survey, Age and Sex (S0101)
2024 1-year estimates of the U.S. population by age and sex, the denominator for the population comparisons (national).
2024
U.S. Department of Education · Office of Special Education Programs
2026 Determination Letters on State Implementation of IDEA
Annual Department determinations for State implementation of IDEA Part B and Part C, based on State Performance Plan and Annual Performance Report submissions. The state snapshot links both the Department page and the 2026 determinations fact sheet PDF.
Federal Fiscal Year 2024 submissions, issued 2026
About this data tool. The Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA) Section 618 requires every state to report data on infants, toddlers, children and youth with disabilities. There are 12 federal collections (8 under Part B, 4 under Part C). This data tool charts Child Count and Educational Environments in depth (School Years 1976–77 through 2024–25), plus headline views of Discipline, Exiting, Personnel, Assessment (Part B), and the Part C child-count, settings and exiting collections for the latest available year. The catalog above links every collection at data.ed.gov.
Data suppression. The Office of Special Education Programs suppresses small or unreliable cells. As defined in the source files: x suppressed, small cell size* suppressed, data-quality concerns– data not available In national aggregates these cells are treated as zero, so affected totals are marginal undercounts; the count and type of suppressed cells appear beneath each affected chart.
Method and scope. Counts cover the 50 states + the District of Columbia (Part C: states + DC), ages 3–21 for Part B and birth–2 for Part C, unless noted. Historical child-count figures (through 2022–23) are from the NCES Digest of Education Statistics 2023, sourced from the Office of Special Education Programs IDEA database; School Year 2023–24 and 2024–25 figures and all other collections were computed directly from the Office of Special Education Programs IDEA Section 618 state-level files (extracted 2024–2025). “Percent of enrollment” reflects 2022–23, the latest year with a published denominator.
Sources: data.ed.gov · IDEA Section 618 Data Products · NCES 204.30 (by disability) · NCES 204.65 (environments) · NCES 204.70 (by state)
